text: When you buy a movie ticket, the theatre barely keeps any of
it. On a big opening weekend, almost all of that money goes straight
back to the studio. The theatre is lucky to keep a few cents on the dollar.
So here's the real question.
If they make almost nothing on the movie, how is the theatre
still open?
The answer is sitting in your lap.
It's the popcorn.
Start with the ticket because it's worse than you think.
When a new blockbuster opens, the studio sets the terms and the
terms are brutal.
For the first couple of weeks, the studio can take most of
the money from every ticket sold.
The theatre's slice only grows the longer the film sticks around, which
is exactly why Some Movie You Forgot existed, is still quietly playing
on screen nine in week six.
That's not leftover scheduling, that's the theatre finally getting paid.
And raising ticket prices barely helps, because about 70 cents of every
extra dollar gets handed right back to the studio too.
So after rent, staff, air conditioning for a giant dark room, and
a projector that costs more than a house, the average theatre runs
on a profit margin of around 4%.
A vending machine in a hospital does better than that, so they
went hunting for the one thing studios don't get a cut of.
Snacks. A serving of popcorn costs the theatre under 50 cents to make.
Kernels are dirt cheap, they puff up to 20 times their size,
and a squirt of fake butter finishes the job.
Then they sell it to you for $9.
That is a markup of nearly 1300%.
And you'll pay it because where exactly are you going to go?
You're sealed in a dark box for two hours, with one snack
counter and a strict no outside food rule.
That's not hospitality.
That's a monopoly the size of a lobby.
The margin on that popcorn sits around 85%.
We don't have to guess at that.
AMC's own financial filings showed more than $800 million in food and
drink sales against about $150 million in cost.
An 81% margin straight off the books of the biggest chain
in the country.
Here's the line that flips the whole thing.
Concessions are only about a fifth of what a theatre takes in,
but they're nearly half of its actual profit.
The movie fills the seats, the popcorn pays the rent.
As one theatre owner whose family had been in the business since
1908 put it, we left the movie business, we're now in the
popcorn business.
And now the part almost nobody knows.
The thing keeping theatres alive today is the exact thing they once
tried to ban. 100 years ago, popcorn was forbidden inside movie theatres.
Owners wanted to feel like grand opera houses, plush carpets, chandeliers, a
certain kind of crowd.
Popcorn was loud, messy and smelled like the street.
So they banned it outright.
Vendors set up carts on the sidewalk and some theatres made you
check your popcorn at the coke counter like a jacket.
Then the Great Depression hit, money disappeared and a movie ticket became
one of the only escapes people could still afford.
A bag of popcorn cost a nickel, that much they could afford.
And the theatres that swallowed their pride and started selling it survived.
The ones too proud to sell it died.
One of the first to really crack it was Julia Braden, a
widow in Kansas City who talked her way into a theatre lobby
and built a popcorn empire while fancier theatres around her went broke.
There was even a Dallas chain that put popcorn machines in 80
of its theatres but refused to put them in its five fanciest
locations because those were too classy for popcorn.
Within two years, the 80 were thriving.
The five holdouts were bleeding money.
Then World War II rationed sugar, which gutted candy and soda.
Popcorn needed no sugar, so it walked in and took the throne.
By 1945, more than half the popcorn eaten in America was eaten
inside a movie theatre.
So here's the real shape of the business.
The movie is the bait, the popcorn is the trap.
The whole building, the lobby, the buttery smell pumped into the air,
the path that funnels you right past the counter, all of it
is engineered to sell you the snack.
The film is just the reason you walked in the door.
So next time you grumble about a $9 popcorn, here's the truth.
You're not overpaying for a snack.
You're paying the theatre's rent.
The ticket was never the point. You got played.
